message:
fix: correct CRC32C convention mismatch in ext4 checksum verification

The Rust `crc32c` crate's `crc32c_append(seed, data)` applies bitwise NOT
to both input and output (matching CRC32C standard finalization), while the
Linux kernel's `crc32c_le(seed, data)` operates on raw CRC register values
with no init/final XOR. This mismatch caused all ext4 metadata checksum
computations (group descriptors, inodes, directory blocks, extent blocks,
superblock) to produce incorrect values.

Add `ext4_chksum(raw_crc, data)` wrapper that bridges the two conventions:
`!crc32c_append(!raw_crc, data)`. Replace all raw `crc32c_append` calls in
ext4 checksum code paths with `ext4_chksum`.

Also in this commit:
- Add `mkfs` subcommand to ffs-cli (creates ext4 images via mkfs.ext4)
- Add 5 FUSE E2E integration tests in ffs-harness
- Add group descriptor CRC32C verification to read_group_desc()
- Implement NFS-style generation number bumping on inode reuse
- Track 9 new beads for remaining work items

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-18gn","title":"RCU/QSBR for lock-free metadata reads (Graveyard Entry 14.8)","description":"# RCU/QSBR for lock-free metadata reads (Graveyard Entry 14.8)

## GOAL
Implement Read-Copy-Update (RCU) with Quiescent-State-Based Reclamation (QSBR) for lock-free metadata reads. Metadata-heavy operations (readdir, stat, lookup) should never block on writers.

## BACKGROUND
From Alien CS Graveyard 14.8: RCU allows readers to access shared data without locks. Writers create new versions and publish them atomically. Old versions are freed after all readers finish (quiescent state detection via QSBR).

## DESIGN
- Protect: inode cache, directory entry cache, extent index lookups
- Read path: no locks, no atomic increments, just dereference current pointer
- Write path: copy-on-write, publish new version via atomic pointer swap
- Reclamation: QSBR — each thread periodically announces quiescent state
- crossbeam-epoch provides QSBR in Rust (same infrastructure as EBR for MVCC)

## APPLICABILITY
- Complements MVCC (RCU for metadata, MVCC for data blocks)
- High-value for workloads with many concurrent readers (web servers, build systems)

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Metadata reads (stat, readdir) never block on concurrent writes
- [ ] No reader-side locks or atomic increments
- [ ] Old metadata versions freed after QSBR quiescent period
- [ ] Benchmark: concurrent stat() throughput vs locked approach","acceptance_criteria":"Metadata reads (stat, readdir) never block on concurrent writes.  {"id":"bd-1b7","title":"Implement btrfs COW B-tree mutation (insert/delete/split/merge)","description":"# Implement btrfs COW B-tree mutation (insert/delete/split/merge)

## GOAL
Implement the core btrfs COW B-tree mutation operations required for write path.

## BACKGROUND
btrfs uses COW semantics for all tree modifications:
- Never modify existing nodes in place
- Create new nodes with modifications
- Update parent pointers up to root
- Old root becomes part of transaction log

This is naturally compatible with MVCC version chains.

## TECHNICAL REQUIREMENTS

### 1. Core Operations
```rust
pub trait BtrfsBTree {
    /// Insert key-value pair, returns new root
    fn insert(&self, key: BtrfsKey, item: &[u8], cx: &Cx) -> Result<BlockNumber>;
    
    /// Delete key, returns new root (or same if not found)
    fn delete(&self, key: &BtrfsKey, cx: &Cx) -> Result<BlockNumber>;
    
    /// Update existing key with new value
    fn update(&self, key: &BtrfsKey, item: &[u8], cx: &Cx) -> Result<BlockNumber>;
    
    /// Range query
    fn range(&self, start: &BtrfsKey, end: &BtrfsKey, cx: &Cx) 
        -> Result<Vec<(BtrfsKey, Vec<u8>)>>;
}
```

### 2. COW Mechanics
```rust
/// Clone-on-write a node with modification
fn cow_node(
    original: BlockNumber,
    modification: NodeMod,
    allocator: &dyn BtrfsAllocator,
    cx: &Cx,
) -> Result<BlockNumber> {
    // 1. Read original node
    // 2. Apply modification (insert/delete/update item)
    // 3. Allocate new block
    // 4. Write modified node to new block
    // 5. Return new block number
}
```

### 3. Split/Merge
- Split when node is full (> max items)
- Merge when node is underfull (< min items)
- Rebalance siblings before merge

### 4. Path Walking
- Track path from root to leaf for COW propagation
- Each level gets new block on modification
- Efficient path reuse for sequential inserts

## TESTS
1. Unit: Insert into empty tree
2. Unit: Insert causing split
3. Unit: Delete causing merge
4. Unit: COW produces new root
5. Integration: 1000 random inserts/deletes, tree invariants hold
6. Property: Random operations maintain sorted order

## LOGGING
- Tree modification (trace): operation, key, old root, new root
- Split/merge events (debug): node level, item counts
- Allocation (trace): new block numbers

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. All operations preserve B-tree invariants
2. COW produces independent tree versions
3. No in-place modification of shared blocks
4.  {"id":"bd-1bx","title":"Implement degradation level display in TUI dashboard","description":"# Implement degradation level display in TUI dashboard

## GOAL
Add degradation level and pressure indicators to the TUI dashboard so operators can monitor system health.

## BACKGROUND
The graceful degradation system (bd-3tz) produces state that must be visible:
- Current degradation level (0-4)
- CPU/memory/IO pressure gauges
- Recent degradation events

## TECHNICAL REQUIREMENTS

### 1. Extended DashboardSnapshot
```rust
pub struct DashboardSnapshot {
    // ... existing fields ...
    
    // Degradation state
    pub degradation_level: DegradationLevel,
    pub cpu_pressure: f64,      // 0.0 - 1.0
    pub memory_pressure: f64,   // 0.0 - 1.0
    pub io_queue_depth: usize,
    pub degradation_events: Vec<DegradationEvent>,
}
```

### 2. New Panel: System Health
```
┌─ System Health ─────────────────┐
│ Degradation: NORMAL (Level 0)   │
│                                 │
│ CPU:    [████████░░] 78%        │
│ Memory: [██████░░░░] 62%        │
│ I/O:    [███░░░░░░░] 34%        │
│                                 │
│ Recent events:                  │
│   12:34:05 Level 0 → 1 (CPU)    │
│   12:35:22 Level 1 → 0          │
└─────────────────────────────────┘
```

### 3. Color Coding
- Level 0 (Normal): Green
- Level 1 (Background paused): Yellow
- Level 2 (Cache reduced): Orange
- Level 3 (Writes throttled): Red
- Level 4 (Read-only): Blinking Red

### 4. Gauge Widgets
```rust
fn render_pressure_gauge(value: f64, width: u16) -> String {
    let filled = (value * width as f64) as u16;
    let bar = "█".repeat(filled as usize) + &"░".repeat((width - filled) as usize);
    format!("[{}] {:>3}%", bar, (value * 100.0) as u8)
}
```

## TESTS
1. Unit: Gauge rendering at various values
2. Unit: Color coding for each level
3. Integration: Dashboard updates on level change
4. Visual: Screenshot test for layout

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. Degradation level always visible
2. Pressure gauges update in real-time
3. Event history shows last 10 events
4.  {"id":"bd-1c4y","title":"EPIC: S3-FIFO for ARC Buffer/Cache Eviction (Graveyard Entry 15.1)","description":"# EPIC: S3-FIFO for ARC Buffer/Cache Eviction (Graveyard Entry 15.1)

## PURPOSE
Replace the current ARC (Adaptive Replacement Cache) buffer cache eviction policy with S3-FIFO, a simpler and often superior eviction algorithm. S3-FIFO uses three FIFO queues — small (admission filter), main, and ghost — with posterior-guided adaptive queue splitting.

## BACKGROUND
From the Alien CS Graveyard entry 15.1:
- S3-FIFO (Yang et al., 2023): three-queue FIFO eviction with admission filter
- Often matches or beats ARC on filesystem workloads with simpler implementation
- No per-access metadata update (unlike ARC which adjusts T1/T2 split on every access)
- Ghost queue provides scan resistance without LRU overhead

## DESIGN

### Three Queues
1. Small Queue (S): admission filter, ~10% of total cache
   - New blocks enter here
   - Evicted from S on first access = not promoted (scan filter)
   - Evicted from S with re-access = promoted to Main
2. Main Queue (M): ~90% of total cache
   - Blocks promoted from S
   - FIFO eviction (no reordering on access)
   - Frequency counter: blocks with count > 0 get second chance
3. Ghost Queue (G): metadata-only (no data), tracks recently evicted
   - Stores block IDs of recently evicted blocks
   - Hit in ghost = block is popular, immediate promotion to Main on re-admission

### Posterior-Guided Adaptive Split
- Bayesian posterior over optimal S/M split ratio
- Update based on ghost hit rate: high ghost hits = S too small
- This extends vanilla S3-FIFO with principled adaptation (alien artifact methodology)

### Drop-In Replacement
- Same BlockCache trait interface as current ARC
- get(block_id) -> Option<BlockData>
- insert(block_id, data) -> Option<(block_id, data)> // evicted entry
- Configurability: total_capacity, small_ratio (default 0.1)

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] S3-FIFO implements BlockCache trait
- [ ] Drop-in replacement for ARC (feature flag to select)
- [ ] Benchmark: hit rate comparison vs ARC on filesystem trace
- [ ] Ghost queue provides scan resistance
- [ ] Adaptive split improves over fixed 10/90 split
- [ ] Unit tests: basic FIFO eviction, promotion, ghost hits

## Success Criteria
1. All children closed
2. E2E cache test (bd-1c4y.3) passes
3. S3-FIFO resists sequential scan pollution
4. Hit rate improvement over plain ARC on mixed workloads
5.  {"id":"bd-1mdk.4","title":"E2E benchmark: Bw-Tree vs locked B-tree extent index throughput","description":"# E2E benchmark: Bw-Tree vs locked B-tree extent index throughput

## GOAL
Prove that the Bw-Tree extent index provides superior concurrent throughput compared to the current locked B-tree implementation. This is the justification benchmark for the entire Bw-Tree epic.

## BENCHMARK SCENARIOS

### Scenario 1: Read-Heavy (95% lookup, 5% insert)
- Simulate metadata-heavy workload (readdir, stat)
- Thread counts: 1, 2, 4, 8, 16
- Measure: lookups/sec, p50/p95/p99 lookup latency
- Expected: Bw-Tree scales linearly (readers never block); locked B-tree saturates

### Scenario 2: Write-Heavy (30% lookup, 70% insert/delete)
- Simulate file creation/deletion workload
- Thread counts: 1, 2, 4, 8, 16
- Measure: operations/sec, CAS retry rate, consolidation frequency
- Expected: Bw-Tree outperforms at >= 4 threads (CAS contention < lock contention)

### Scenario 3: Mixed Workload (realistic filesystem)
- 50% lookup, 30% insert, 10% delete, 10% range scan
- Thread counts: 1, 2, 4, 8, 16
- Measure: aggregate ops/sec, per-operation latency breakdown
- Expected: Bw-Tree advantage increases with thread count

### Scenario 4: Single-Threaded Baseline
- Pure sequential operations (no concurrency)
- Measure: Bw-Tree overhead vs B-tree (delta chain traversal cost)
- Expected: B-tree faster single-threaded (simpler structure); Bw-Tree within 2x

### Scenario 5: Consolidation Overhead
- Measure: consolidation frequency, duration, and impact on concurrent operations
- Stress test: maximum write rate with consolidation enabled/disabled
- Expected: consolidation adds < 10% overhead at steady state

## METRICS

 {"id":"bd-29z.1","title":"Implement btrfs extent allocation for write path","description":"# Implement btrfs extent allocation for write path

## GOAL
Implement block/extent allocation for btrfs write operations, mapping the extent tree allocation model.

## BACKGROUND
btrfs allocates extents via the extent tree (EXTENT_ITEM / METADATA_ITEM keys). Unlike ext4's bitmap-per-group, btrfs uses a B-tree of free space (free space cache or free space tree).

For V1 single-device, we need:
1. Find free space (scan extent tree for gaps or use free space tree)
2. Allocate extent (insert EXTENT_ITEM)
3. Update block group counters
4. Track in delayed-ref system for transaction commit

## DELIVERABLES
1. `BtrfsAllocator` struct managing extent allocation
2. Free space lookup (gap-based or free-space-tree)
3. Extent insertion into extent tree
4. Block group accounting
5. Integration with delayed-ref tracking (bd-3sj)

## TESTS
1. Allocate single extent in empty group
2. Allocate fills group, next allocation goes to next group
3. Free extent creates reclaimable space
4. Allocation respects block group type (data vs metadata)

## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS
Every allocation operation MUST emit structured logs with:
- `[BTRFS:Alloc:Search]` - free space search: `debug!(target: "ffs::btrfs::alloc", block_group=BG, type=T, size_needed=S, "alloc_search_start")`
- `[BTRFS:Alloc:Found]` - found free extent: `debug!(target: "ffs::btrfs::alloc", block_group=BG, extent_start=E, extent_size=S, "alloc_found")`
- `[BTRFS:Alloc:Insert]` - insert EXTENT_ITEM: `info!(target: "ffs::btrfs::alloc", bytenr=B, size=S, type=T, refs=1, "extent_item_insert")`
- `[BTRFS:Alloc:Accounting]` - block group accounting: `trace!(target: "ffs::btrfs::alloc", block_group=BG, used_before=U1, used_after=U2, delta=D, "bg_accounting")`
- `[BTRFS:Alloc:DelayedRef]` - delayed ref created: `debug!(target: "ffs::btrfs::alloc", bytenr=B, ref_type=T, action="add", "delayed_ref_queue")`
- `[BTRFS:Free:Search]` - free extent search: `debug!(target: "ffs::btrfs::alloc", bytenr=B, size=S, "free_search")`
- `[BTRFS:Free:Remove]` - remove EXTENT_ITEM: `info!(target: "ffs::btrfs::alloc", bytenr=B, size=S, "extent_item_remove")`

Log levels:
- TRACE: accounting updates (high volume)
- DEBUG: search operations, delayed-ref queuing
- INFO: actual extent inserts/removes (key events)
- WARN: allocation fallback (group full, try next)
- ERROR: allocation failure (no space)

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. Can allocate extents for file data writes
2. Free space accounting is consistent
3. Works with transaction model (bd-tbn)
4. All allocations logged and traceable","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:52:08.735693297Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T20:35:16.810978060Z","closed_at":"2026-02-13T20:35:16.810951922Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-29z.1","depends_on_id":"bd-1b7","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:52:08.876614461Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-29z.1","depends_on_id":"bd-29z","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:52:08.735693297Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":45,"issue_id":"bd-29z.1","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"BTRFS SPECIFICS: Unlike ext4's bitmap-per-group, btrfs tracks free space through the extent tree itself.  {"id":"bd-2ad","title":"Implement FUSE mount lifecycle (mount/unmount/signal handling)","description":"# Implement FUSE mount lifecycle (mount/unmount/signal handling)

## GOAL
Implement robust mount lifecycle management with clean startup, shutdown, and signal handling.

## BACKGROUND
Production filesystems must handle:
- Clean mount with validation
- Graceful unmount with flush
- Signal handling (SIGTERM, SIGINT)
- Crash recovery on restart

## TECHNICAL REQUIREMENTS

### 1. Mount Phase
```rust
pub async fn mount(config: MountConfig, cx: &Cx) -> Result<MountHandle> {
    // 1. Validate image (superblock, journal state)
    // 2. Replay journal if needed (ext4)
    // 3. Initialize MVCC store
    // 4. Initialize ARC cache
    // 5. Initialize repair manager
    // 6. Start background tasks (scrub, GC)
    // 7. Mount FUSE session
    // 8. Return handle for lifecycle control
}
```

### 2. Unmount Phase
```rust
impl MountHandle {
    pub async fn unmount(self, cx: &Cx) -> Result<()> {
        // 1. Stop accepting new requests
        // 2. Wait for in-flight requests (with timeout)
        // 3. Flush dirty cache entries
        // 4. Commit pending transactions
        // 5. Refresh repair symbols for dirty groups
        // 6. Close FUSE session
        // 7. Sync and close device
    }
}
```

### 3. Signal Handling
- SIGTERM/SIGINT: Trigger graceful unmount
- SIGUSR1: Dump debug state to log
- Timeout for unmount (default 30s, then force)

### 4. Drop Safety
- MountHandle::drop must trigger unmount
- Panic in unmount must not corrupt filesystem
- Leaked handle must eventually timeout

## TESTS
1. Unit: Mount/unmount roundtrip
2. Integration: SIGTERM during I/O results in clean state
3. Integration: Double-mount same image fails cleanly
4. Stress: Mount/unmount loop 100x without leaks

## LOGGING
- Mount: log config, image path, mount point
- Unmount: log flush stats, pending ops
- Signal: log signal received, action taken

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. `ffs mount ... && ffs umount ...` leaves clean state
2. `kill -TERM $(pidof ffs)` results in clean unmount
3. Remount after crash shows no corruption
4. No resource leaks (file descriptors, memory)","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T02:50:34.171965858Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T10:14:20.310382109Z","closed_at":"2026-02-13T10:14:20.310314994Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["fuse","lifecycle","reliability"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2ad","depends_on_id":"bd-20p","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T02:54:06.032850244Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2ad","depends_on_id":"bd-2s4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:56:37.263209229Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":26,"issue_id":"bd-2ad","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"FEATURE_PARITY mapping: This bead covers the 'FUSE mount runtime' row (currently ❌).  {"id":"bd-32yn","title":"EPIC: Testing & Fault Injection","description":"# EPIC: Testing & Fault Injection

## PURPOSE
Build a comprehensive fault injection and stress testing framework for FrankenFS. A filesystem that cannot prove it handles failures correctly is not production-worthy. This epic covers: WAL interruption, partial repair, metadata corruption, I/O throttling, crash-replay testing, fsx-style stress, and xfstests compatibility.

## BACKGROUND
Kernel filesystems (ext4, btrfs, XFS) have decades of testing infrastructure: xfstests, fsx, blktests, syzkaller. FrankenFS must build equivalent coverage, with the added dimension of testing its unique features (MVCC, RaptorQ self-healing).

## SCOPE
1. Fault injection primitives (pluggable I/O layer with configurable failures)
2. WAL interruption tests (crash during journal write)
3. Partial repair tests (incomplete RaptorQ symbols)
4. Metadata corruption tests (corrupt specific metadata structures)
5. I/O throttling tests (slow I/O, deadline misses)
6. Crash-replay suite (deterministic crash schedules)
7. fsx-style stress (random operations + verification)
8. xfstests compatibility (run standard FS test suites)

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Fault injection layer can inject failures at any I/O point
- [ ] All fault injection tests are deterministic and CI-executable
- [ ] Crash-replay covers 500+ random schedules
- [ ] fsx-style stress runs for configurable duration with corruption checking
- [ ] xfstests generic + ext4 subsets pass

## Success Criteria
1. All children closed
2. Fault injection framework reusable across all scenarios
3. xfstests subset passes (bd-32yn.7)
4. Crash-replay suite covers 500+ schedules
5.  {"id":"bd-cebm","title":"Implement FUSE fsync/flush with durability guarantees","description":"# Implement FUSE fsync/flush with durability guarantees

## GOAL
Implement correct fsync and flush semantics for FUSE mount, ensuring durability guarantees match user expectations.

## BACKGROUND
fsync is the primary durability contract:
- `fsync(fd)` must ensure all data written to fd is on stable storage
- `fdatasync(fd)` same but may skip non-essential metadata
- `flush` is called on close (cleanup, not durability)

## CRITICAL REQUIREMENTS

### 1. fsync Semantics
```rust
impl FuseBackend {
    fn fsync(&self, ino: u64, fh: u64, datasync: bool, reply: fuser::ReplyEmpty) {
        // 1. Get inode from fh
        // 2. Commit any pending MVCC transaction for this file
        // 3. Flush dirty cache entries for this inode's blocks
        // 4. If !datasync: also flush inode metadata block
        // 5. Wait for I/O completion
        // 6. If repair_eager: refresh RaptorQ symbols for dirty groups
        // 7. reply.ok() or reply.error(errno)
    }
}
```

### 2. Flush Semantics
```rust
impl FuseBackend {
    fn flush(&self, ino: u64, fh: u64, lock_owner: u64, reply: fuser::ReplyEmpty) {
        // Called on close - release resources, no durability guarantee
        // 1. Release file handle
        // 2. Decrement open count
        // 3. If last close + unlinked: queue for deletion
        // 4. reply.ok()
    }
}
```

### 3. fsyncdir Semantics
```rust
impl FuseBackend {
    fn fsyncdir(&self, ino: u64, fh: u64, datasync: bool, reply: fuser::ReplyEmpty) {
        // Same as fsync but for directories
        // Ensures directory entries are durable
    }
}
```

### 4. Integration Points
- **MVCC**: fsync triggers transaction commit
- **ARC Cache**: fsync flushes dirty entries for inode
- **RaptorQ**: fsync may trigger eager symbol refresh
- **Journal**: fsync ensures journal commit block written

### 5. Error Handling
- I/O error during fsync -> EROFS or EIO
- MVCC conflict -> EAGAIN (retry)
- Timeout -> EIO with detailed logging

## TESTS (REQUIRED)
1. Unit: fsync flushes dirty blocks for inode
2. Unit: fdatasync skips metadata
3. Integration: fsync + crash + recovery -> data durable
4. Integration: fsync on RO mount -> EROFS
5. E2E: Write + fsync + unmount + remount -> data present

## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS
- fsync start (debug): inode, datasync flag
- fsync cache flush (trace): block count, dirty bytes
- fsync commit (trace): commit_seq
- fsync complete (debug): duration_us, blocks_flushed
- fsync error (warn): inode, error, context

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. fsync guarantees data is on stable storage
2. fsync failure returns correct errno
3. No data loss if process crashes after successful fsync
4.  {"id":"bd-xzn0","title":"Succinct structures for free-space bitmaps (Graveyard Entry 7.1)","description":"# Succinct structures for free-space bitmaps (Graveyard Entry 7.1)

## GOAL
Replace traditional free-space bitmaps with succinct data structures (rank/select bitmaps) that support O(1) rank and select queries while using near-optimal space.

## BACKGROUND
From Alien CS Graveyard 7.1: Succinct bitmaps (Jacobson 1989, Clark 1996) store bitmaps in n + o(n) bits while supporting:
- rank(i): count set bits in positions [0, i) — O(1)
- select(k): find position of k-th set bit — O(1)

For free-space bitmaps, this enables:
- O(1) "how many free blocks in range?" (rank)
- O(1) "find k-th free block" (select) — replaces linear scan for allocation

## DESIGN
- Two-level rank structure: superblocks (every 2^16 bits) + blocks (every 2^9 bits)
- Select via rank binary search or Vigna's select structure
- Space overhead: ~6.25% over raw bitmap (vs 0% for plain bitmap, but queries are O(n))
- Integration: replace ffs-ext4 block bitmap and inode bitmap

## APPLICABILITY
- Optimization for large filesystems where bitmap scans are expensive
- Most impactful when block groups are large or filesystem is >90% full
- Could also accelerate mballoc buddy allocator queries

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Succinct bitmap with O(1) rank and select
- [ ] Space overhead < 10% over raw bitmap
- [ ] Benchmark: allocation scan time vs plain bitmap
- [ ] Drop-in replacement for block/inode bitmaps","acceptance_criteria":"Succinct bitmap with O(1) rank and select operations. Space overhead below 10% over raw bitmap. Two-level rank structure: superblocks (every 2^16 bits) plus blocks (every 2^9 bits). Drop-in replacement for block and inode bitmaps via BlockBitmap trait. Benchmark: allocation scan time vs plain bitmap at 50%, 80%, 95% fullness. Benchmark: rank/select latency vs linear scan. Unit tests: rank correctness for all-zeros, all-ones, random patterns. Unit tests: select correctness for sparse and dense bitmaps. Unit tests: round-trip serialization/deserialization. Property test: rank(select(k)) == k for all valid k.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CoralThrush","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:30:45.958783809Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-18T06:30:55.736127822Z","closed_at":"2026-02-18T06:30:55.736029688Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["datastructure","freespace","succinct"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-xzn0","depends_on_id":"bd-3ib","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:05:32.714682331Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":202,"issue_id":"bd-xzn0","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Alien uplift contract (auto-applied via extreme-software-optimization + alien-artifact-coding + alien-graveyard).
